task.user_request: i want to review the architecture the app, which is the app that is running right now and allow the group to suggest a better structure for speed, startup by the user and ease of distribution, where exe and dmg would be the ultimate distribution method.
task.user_decision: the consultation is massive, i will take all recommendations and review all of them to determine which path to pursue if any.

structured.analysis:
Desired artifact: an architecture recommendation and migration plan for the currently running Conclave AI app, focused on startup speed, user startup ergonomics, and distribution as exe/dmg. Audience: Glen and the implementation agents. Success criteria: faster perceived launch, fewer user-visible setup steps, reproducible packaging, recoverable local state, and no degradation to observability/durability/audit trail/retention/export. Constraints: static review only in this round; read-only files are available but command execution and file edits are not granted; therefore I cannot benchmark startup, inspect live process behavior, or validate packager behavior. Time horizon: near-term desktop distribution path with room for later hardening. Risk priorities: avoid losing task history, secrets, logs, exports, decision records, or debuggability while packaging. Permission boundaries: no commands, no edits, no installs, no patches.

My first-pass architectural read from the manifest: the app is already modular in broad layers: app/api exposes HTTP endpoints; app/services owns orchestration, persistence support, exports, retention, sandboxing, prompt building, and PID locking; app/agents owns adapters; app/dashboard is a static browser UI; app/workers has task worker logic; app/database.py centralizes storage. This is a solid local-service architecture for an MVP, but not yet obviously optimized as a consumer desktop app. Large single frontend files such as dashboard.js and dashboard.css suggest the UI is bundled manually rather than through a build system. The Python backend likely starts the web server, opens/serves the dashboard, loads settings/config, sets PID locks, prepares DB, and invokes adapters. That is reasonable for development, but packaging it directly can produce slow cold starts and brittle distribution if the backend, UI assets, config, logs, database, and external CLI dependencies are not clearly separated.

Primary recommendation: keep the core backend as the source of truth, but introduce a desktop-host architecture with three explicit layers. Layer 1: switchboard-core, pure Python domain/service code containing orchestration, protocol validation, adapters, persistence, retention, exports, and decision memory. Layer 2: local-server-host, a small Python entrypoint responsible only for config discovery, migrations, PID locking, DB path setup, log setup, health check, and starting FastAPI. Layer 3: desktop-shell, a distributable app wrapper that launches/supervises the local server, opens an embedded webview or browser window, displays startup/progress/errors, and provides quit/restart/open logs/export support. For the MVP, this desktop shell could be intentionally thin; the key structural change is making launch supervision and user-facing startup state first-class rather than incidental.

For speed and startup: optimize for perceived launch first. The desktop shell should show a native or lightweight loading window immediately, then wait for /health before loading the dashboard. Backend startup should defer nonessential work: do not initialize all agent adapters, scan large docs, or perform retention/export work before the UI is usable. Load adapters lazily when selected or when a task requires them. Move expensive startup checks behind explicit health detail endpoints. Add structured startup phases: config loaded, database ready, server listening, dashboard ready, adapters available. This gives users feedback and gives support logs useful evidence.

For user startup: avoid requiring the user to run Python, uvicorn, or scripts. The installed app should own one-click launch, single-instance behavior, local data directory creation, and clean shutdown. Config and secrets should live in OS-appropriate user data locations, not beside the executable or inside the app bundle. The existing settings panel and database API key work appears directionally useful here, but packaging must preserve exportability and recovery. The app should include an obvious way to open logs, open data folder, export full task history, and reset only nonessential cached state.

For distribution: I would not start by rewriting the backend into Electron or a different stack. A conservative path is Python backend packaged by PyInstaller or Briefcase-style tooling plus a lightweight desktop shell. On Windows, ship an installer that places an exe, creates Start Menu shortcuts, and uses the user profile for app data. On macOS, ship a signed/notarized app inside a dmg, again with user data outside the app bundle. The dashboard can remain web-based. If a native shell is needed, pywebview is a plausible thin wrapper; Tauri/Electron are also options, but they increase build complexity. Given the charter's operability principle, the lowest-risk architecture is to preserve the service boundary and add better supervision around it before changing UI frameworks.

Operability impact: this recommendation improves observability by creating explicit startup phases, health checks, logs, and user-visible error states. It improves durability/recoverability by separating immutable app bundle files from mutable user data and by preserving exports. It improves audit trail and retention if packaging includes stable data paths and does not hide logs inside temporary bundle locations. It increases build complexity, especially for Windows/macOS signing and external adapter dependencies, but the complexity is bounded and directly tied to distribution. I would reject a large rewrite to Electron/Tauri-native backend at this stage because it risks displacing known operability foundations without first proving startup and packaging gains.

What I would ask the consultants to pressure-test: whether pywebview/thin wrapper is enough or whether a pure browser-open local service is better for MVP; which packaging tool best fits the Python dependencies; whether current adapter dependencies on external CLIs make a self-contained exe/dmg unrealistic; and what startup path can be measured with the least instrumentation.

recommended_actions.description: Perform a static architecture review of the current FastAPI/service/dashboard structure, identifying startup-critical paths, packaging boundaries, mutable data paths, and external runtime dependencies.
Adopt a three-layer target structure for distribution planning: switchboard-core, local-server-host, and desktop-shell, while preserving the existing backend and dashboard as much as possible.
Define a startup contract with explicit phases, a fast /health endpoint, deferred adapter initialization, stable log/data/config directories, and user-facing startup error states.
In a later permissioned pass, measure cold-start time, import cost, server-ready time, dashboard-ready time, and adapter initialization cost before choosing packaging tooling.

Compare PyInstaller plus thin shell, Briefcase, pywebview, Tauri, Electron, and browser-open local service specifically against Windows exe and macOS dmg distribution, signing/notarization, startup time, dependency bundling, and supportability.
If Glen accepts the direction, create a decision record documenting the packaging architecture choice, rejected alternatives, startup goals, and Operability Impact.

risks.description: Static-only review cannot confirm actual startup bottlenecks; import-time cost, database migration cost, adapter initialization, and dashboard asset load need measurement before implementation choices are final.
External agent adapters may depend on installed CLIs, credentials, model-provider tools, or network availability; a self-contained exe/dmg may still need clear dependency checks and degraded modes.

Packaging Python apps can accidentally place mutable state inside install directories or temporary extraction folders, harming durability, recoverability, retention, and export unless data paths are explicitly designed.

A heavier shell such as Electron or Tauri may improve desktop polish but adds build, update, signing, and debugging complexity; that conflicts with operability unless the benefit is concrete and measured.
Large dashboard assets may slow first render or make packaging noisy, but this is likely secondary to backend/import/adapter startup until measured.

structured.summary: I recommend treating this as a packaging-and-runtime architecture review, not just a code organization review: the goal is a fast local desktop app that can start reliably for a user and ship as Windows exe and macOS dmg. Based on the manifest, the current app appears to be a Python FastAPI backend with static dashboard assets, SQLite/local persistence, subprocess or API-based agent adapters, and client-side slash-command integrations; the likely improvement path is to separate core orchestration from the local desktop host and make the desktop distribution a thin, supervised runtime around the existing service.

structured.critique: The primary proposal provides a solid architectural direction focusing on startup speed, user experience, and distribution. However, it overlooks some critical aspects: 1) The impact of external CLI dependencies on packaging integrity and startup reliability needs deeper analysis. For instance, running external CLIs like `codex` or `claude` could introduce startup delays or failures if those tools aren't properly initialized or available in the packaged environment. 2) The proposal mentions PyInstaller and Briefcase as packaging options but doesn't adequately address how these tools handle external dependencies or ensure consistent behavior across different environments. 3) The separation into three layers (switchboard-core, local-server-host, desktop-shell) is a good approach, but the transition plan and potential migration risks aren't discussed. For example, how will existing configurations, logs, and task histories be migrated to the new structure while ensuring continuity? 4) The proposal could benefit from more specific metrics for startup speed optimization, such as target milliseconds for each phase (config loaded, database ready, server listening, etc.).
missed_risks: External CLI dependencies may cause startup failures or delays in packaged environments.
Packaging tools may not handle external dependencies consistently across environments.
Migration to a three-layer structure could introduce configuration and data continuity risks.
suggested_questions: How will external CLI dependencies be managed in a packaged environment to ensure reliable startup?
What specific metrics should be targeted for startup speed optimization?

structured.critique: The 3-layer architecture (core, server host, shell) is exactly the right path, but the specific packaging technologies suggested undermine the stated goals. First, PyInstaller is explicitly the wrong choice for 'faster perceived launch'. It extracts its payload to a temporary directory (_MEIPASS) on every cold start, introducing seconds of delay before the first line of Python even executes. We should instead use an embeddable Python distribution, Nuitka (which compiles to C for speedups), or PyOxidizer to run directly from disk/memory. Second, `pywebview` is brittle across OS versions and causes frequent distribution support headaches. A much more robust 'thin shell' for an MVP is a lightweight system tray executable (e.g., using Go, Rust/Tauri, or PyStray) that orchestrates the backend and opens the user's default OS browser to the local port. Finally, running a local web server introduces port collision risks; the host layer must dynamically bind to an available port and pass it to the UI shell.
missed_risks: Port collisions: If the target backend port is in use, the app will fail to start. The server must dynamically bind a free port and communicate it to the shell.
PyInstaller cold start penalty: PyInstaller's extraction overhead directly contradicts the goal of rapid startup.
Improper termination: Desktop users frequently force-quit apps; FastAPI and SQLite must handle abrupt termination without data corruption.
suggested_questions: Should we prioritize a native tray icon that opens the default OS browser over bundling a dedicated webview, to reduce bundle size and avoid compatibility issues?
If we avoid PyInstaller to improve cold start speed, does Nuitka or an embeddable Python structure better fit our CI pipeline for Windows and macOS?

structured.critique:
Strong direction on the three-layer architecture and operability-first thinking. The principle of separating app bundle from mutable user data, explicit startup phases, and health checks before UI load is sound and aligns with the Charter's operability principle. However, the proposal underestimates three critical blockers that will dominate the actual implementation:

**1. External CLI dependency is the real blocker, not packaging.** The entire adapter layer (codex_adapter, gemini_adapter, claude_adapter) wraps subprocess calls to external CLIs. These are not Python dependencies — you cannot bundle them into an exe/dmg. The proposal mentions this as "a high-severity risk" but then moves forward without a concrete solution. For the distribution goal to work, you need: (a) a strategy for detecting/checking CLI availability at runtime, (b) explicit degradation modes when a CLI is missing, (c) either bundled CLI shims or a clear user-facing guide that says "install Codex separately first." None of these are addressed. PyInstaller won't solve this — it only bundles Python. The adapter layer is the load-bearing wall for the packaging path, and it needs its own design pass.

**2. Startup bottleneck is unidentified.** The proposal calls for lazy loading adapters and deferred initialization, but adapter instantiation is probably not the critical path. Reading the source: `agent_registry.init_registry()` and `register_openrouter_models()` just instantiate adapter objects; they don't I/O. The real startup cost is likely (a) Python import time (FastAPI, Pydantic, etc.), (b) SQLite open + WAL setup + busy_timeout negotiation, (c) orphan reaper scan (DB query), (d) sandbox cleanup (filesystem walk). Defer adapter init, sure, but that's maybe 5–15% of startup time. Without a measurement pass (which the proposal acknowledges is not possible in read-only mode), you risk doing a three-layer refactor for gains that aren't there. Suggest: measure first. Cold start a current uvicorn instance and profile with `py-spy` or `cProfile` to identify the actual bottleneck. That data should drive the architecture, not the other way around.

**3. Config path refactoring is deferred but blocks packaging.** The app currently reads `config.yaml` from cwd or `SWITCHBOARD_CONFIG` env var. For a packaged app to work, config must move to OS-appropriate user-data directories: `~/Library/Application Support/AI Switchboard/` on macOS, `%APPDATA%/AI Switchboard/` on Windows. The proposal says "config and secrets should live in OS-appropriate user data locations" but doesn't specify the implementation. `app/config.py:load_config()` has to change, startup has to handle config discovery and migration, and the installer has to seed initial config. This is a real refactor that the proposal does not scope.

**Strong agreement:**
- Three-layer split (core/host/shell) reduces coupling and is correct long-term.
- Explicit startup phases with `phase_status` is excellent for observability and user feedback.
- Health endpoint before dashboard load is the right optimization priority.
- Preserving exports and decision memory as externally-auditable artifacts is essential — packaging must not hide those.
- Conservative path (PyInstaller or pywebview, not Electron) is pragmatic.

**Risks the proposal identifies but doesn't resolve:**
- "External agent adapters may depend on installed CLIs" — yes, and this is the blocker for self-contained exe/dmg, not a secondary concern.
- "Packaging Python apps can accidentally place mutable state inside install directories" — true, and this is more likely without explicit path design upfront.

**Questions for the primary:**
- Before the three-layer refactor, can you baseline actual cold-start time? Is it actually slow, or is the goal "more user-friendly than running a command"?
- If external CLIs are unavailable, should Switchboard degrade gracefully (OpenRouter-only mode, fake adapter, or hard fail)? This shapes the adapter architecture.
- Is the MVP distribution really "exe/dmg that ship with zero setup," or is "Python backend + one-click launcher that assumes CLIs are on PATH" acceptable as a first step?
- Where should config live after packaging? (This determines app/config.py and installer design.)
missed_risks: External CLI adapters (Codex, Gemini, Claude) cannot be bundled in a self-contained exe/dmg; they remain external dependencies. Packaging strategy must explicitly address detection, degradation, and user guidance or the distribution goal will fail.

Startup bottleneck is speculative without measurement. Lazy-loading adapters, config parsing, and startup phases are good practices, but if the real cost is Python import time or SQLite WAL negotiation, the three-layer refactor may not yield the perceived startup gains.
Config path refactoring is glossed over but blocks packaging. `load_config()` reads from cwd/env-var; OS-appropriate paths require app/config.py changes, installer logic, and migration handling.

Packaged Python apps (PyInstaller, Briefcase) have their own operability risks: brittle updates, certificate management for signing, bundle size (Python stdlib is large), and version skew between bundled and system Python. These deserve explicit risk documentation.
No degradation strategy if external CLI is missing or fails auth. Adapters will error; should the app fall back to OpenRouter-only mode, or should it fail startup?
suggested_questions: Before committing to a three-layer refactor, can you measure actual cold-start time? Profile with `cProfile` or `py-spy` to identify the real bottleneck (Python imports, DB init, orphan reaper, or something else). This data should drive the architecture, not speculation.

If a user installs the exe but doesn't have Codex/Gemini/Claude on their PATH, what happens? Does Switchboard fail to start, or does it degrade to OpenRouter-only mode? This determines whether the packaging path is blocked on external CLI bundling.

Is the distribution MVP really "fully self-contained exe/dmg," or is "Python backend + OS-native launcher that assumes CLIs are available" acceptable? The former requires solving the adapter dependency problem; the latter is simpler and may be good-enough for a first release.

Where should config live in the packaged app? Specify the full path logic for Windows (%APPDATA%) and macOS (~/.../Application Support/). Should the installer create these directories? Should the app migrate config from old locations?

structured.analysis:
I accept the core consultant critique: my first proposal correctly identified the three-layer direction, but treated external CLIs and config paths as risks rather than first-class design blockers. The final recommendation is therefore more specific: distribution should be designed around a supervised local backend plus explicit dependency readiness, not around pretending the exe/dmg can fully contain Codex, Claude Code, Gemini, credentials, and provider-specific tooling.

Current architecture, based on the manifest and prior static review, appears to be a Python FastAPI app with static dashboard assets, SQLite/local persistence, service modules for orchestration/export/retention/sandboxing, and adapters that include subprocess-backed CLIs plus API-backed seats such as OpenRouter. That is a reasonable MVP foundation. I would not rewrite the backend or dashboard stack just to get desktop distribution. The better structure is: 1. switchboard-core: protocol, orchestration, adapters, persistence, retention, export, decision memory, and sandbox abstractions; 2. local-server-host: config discovery, data-directory setup, migrations, PID/single-instance behavior, dynamic port binding, startup phase reporting, health endpoints, log setup, graceful shutdown; 3. desktop-launcher: OS-native executable/app bundle that starts or attaches to the host, shows immediate startup state, opens the UI in the user's browser for MVP, exposes tray/menu actions for open app, restart backend, open logs, open data folder, export, and quit.

I accept Gemini's point on port collisions. A packaged app should not assume a fixed port. The host should bind to an available localhost port, write the active port to a runtime file or hand it directly to the launcher, and ensure browser/dashboard URLs use that actual port. Single-instance behavior should route a second launch to the already-running instance rather than starting another backend.

I partially accept Gemini's packaging critique. PyInstaller one-file can have a cold-start extraction penalty and should not be the default if speed is a priority. But saying PyInstaller is categorically wrong is too broad because one-dir packaging behaves differently and may still be useful for internal builds. The packaging comparison should explicitly benchmark PyInstaller one-dir, Nuitka, Python embeddable distribution, PyOxidizer, and Briefcase against cold start, signing/notarization friction, bundle size, update behavior, and supportability. For the shell, I accept the argument that a browser/tray launcher is the better MVP than pywebview. A webview can be revisited later if the default-browser experience is unacceptable.

I accept Claude's point that external CLI adapters are the load-bearing wall. The packaged app should start even when Codex, Claude Code, or Gemini CLIs are missing or unauthenticated. Missing adapters should be reported in an Agents/Readiness panel with clear install/auth guidance and should disable only those seats. OpenRouter/API-backed seats and fake/test seats can remain available if configured. Startup should not block on CLI checks; readiness checks should run asynchronously after the UI is usable. This preserves operability and avoids turning distribution into a brittle all-or-nothing installer.

I accept the startup-measurement critique. The architecture should not assume lazy adapter construction is the main bottleneck. The first permissioned implementation pass should baseline cold and warm launch: launcher visible time, Python process start, imports, config load, DB/WAL setup, orphan reaper, sandbox cleanup, server listening, /health OK, first dashboard render, and async adapter readiness. Until measured, practical target budgets should be treated as provisional: launcher visible under 500 ms, /health ready under 2 s warm and under 5 s cold on a normal developer laptop, dashboard interactive under 3 s warm and under 7 s cold, and adapter readiness checks completed asynchronously within roughly 5-10 s without blocking core UI.

I accept Claude's config-path critique. Packaging is blocked until mutable state is moved out of the install/app bundle. The host should resolve OS-specific directories: on Windows, config/data/logs under a user profile location such as %APPDATA% or %LOCALAPPDATA% depending on roaming needs; on macOS, under ~/Library/Application Support/<app name> with logs also available through a user-accessible logs path. The existing cwd/config.yaml behavior should become a development fallback or explicit override, not the packaged default. Migration should copy or import existing config, task DB, exports, and decision records once, leave the original intact, and record the migration in logs.

Operability impact: this structure strengthens observability through startup phases, health/readiness endpoints, runtime-port reporting, and user-accessible logs. It strengthens durability and recoverability by separating immutable bundled app files from mutable DB/config/log/export state. It preserves audit trail, retention, and export if task history and decision records remain in stable user-data paths and export remains accessible from the launcher/UI. It does increase build and release complexity, especially for Windows/macOS signing and dependency readiness UX, but that complexity is directly tied to distribution and is bounded by keeping the backend and dashboard architecture largely intact.

Final structure recommendation: do not pursue a self-contained zero-dependency exe/dmg as the immediate promise. Ship a one-click desktop distribution that bundles the Switchboard backend and dashboard, starts fast enough, degrades gracefully when external agent CLIs are missing, and clearly reports readiness. Treat fully bundled third-party CLIs, embedded webview, and auto-update as later decisions after the host layer, path model, and startup measurements are solid.

recommended_actions.description: Adopt the three-layer desktop distribution architecture: switchboard-core, local-server-host, and desktop-launcher/browser shell, while preserving the existing FastAPI backend and static dashboard for the first packaged release.

payload.mvp_shell: native launcher or tray app that opens the default browser

recommended_actions.description: Define the local-server-host contract: OS-specific config/data/log paths, dynamic localhost port binding, single-instance behavior, startup phase reporting, fast health endpoint, readiness endpoint, graceful shutdown, and user-visible failure states.

Create an adapter dependency/readiness model so missing Codex, Claude Code, or Gemini CLIs do not prevent the app from starting; unavailable seats should be disabled with clear install/auth guidance while available API-backed seats continue to work.

payload.startup_behavior: asynchronous readiness checks after core UI loads

recommended_actions.description: In a later permissioned pass, measure current cold and warm startup before refactoring so startup work targets the real bottlenecks.

Benchmark packaging options against startup speed and operability: Python embeddable distribution, Nuitka, PyOxidizer, Briefcase, and PyInstaller one-dir; avoid PyInstaller one-file as the default until its cold-start cost is proven acceptable.
Design config/data migration from cwd/config.yaml and existing local project paths into OS user-data locations, preserving original files and recording migration evidence in logs.

migration_requirements: support explicit config override for development

recommended_actions.description: If Glen accepts this direction, create a decision record for desktop distribution architecture including the Operability Impact field required by the charter.

risks.description: External CLI-backed adapters cannot be assumed to work inside a packaged exe/dmg. Without a readiness/degradation model, the desktop app may fail for users who have not separately installed or authenticated Codex, Claude Code, or Gemini tooling.

No command execution is granted in this task, so all startup bottleneck claims remain static hypotheses. Actual cold-start profiling requires can_run_commands permission before implementation priorities are final.
Moving config, logs, databases, exports, and decision records to OS user-data paths can break continuity if migration is not explicit, non-destructive, and logged.
A fixed localhost port can collide with another process and make the packaged app appear broken. Dynamic port binding and single-instance handoff are required.
Packaging choices affect operability. One-file bundle extraction, signing/notarization failures, opaque crash logs, or mutable state inside app bundles can undermine startup speed and recoverability.
A browser-based MVP is more robust than an embedded webview but may feel less like a native app. That is an acceptable first tradeoff if startup, logging, export, and adapter readiness are stronger.
Large dashboard JS/CSS files may affect first render and bundle size, but they should be measured before becoming a refactor target.

structured.summary: The best target architecture is a local-service desktop app: keep the existing FastAPI/orchestration core, add a small host layer for startup, paths, health, logging, and supervision, and ship a thin desktop launcher/tray app that opens the local UI. The critical blockers are not the UI wrapper itself but external CLI adapter dependencies, config/data path migration, dynamic port handling, and measured cold-start behavior.

final_result.final_answer:
The best target architecture is a local-service desktop app: keep the existing FastAPI/orchestration core, add a small host layer for startup, paths, health, logging, and supervision, and ship a thin desktop launcher/tray app that opens the local UI. The critical blockers are not the UI wrapper itself but external CLI adapter dependencies, config/data path migration, dynamic port handling, and measured cold-start behavior.

I accept the core consultant critique: my first proposal correctly identified the three-layer direction, but treated external CLIs and config paths as risks rather than first-class design blockers. The final recommendation is therefore more specific: distribution should be designed around a supervised local backend plus explicit dependency readiness, not around pretending the exe/dmg can fully contain Codex, Claude Code, Gemini, credentials, and provider-specific tooling.

Current architecture, based on the manifest and prior static review, appears to be a Python FastAPI app with static dashboard assets, SQLite/local persistence, service modules for orchestration/export/retention/sandboxing, and adapters that include subprocess-backed CLIs plus API-backed seats such as OpenRouter. That is a reasonable MVP foundation. I would not rewrite the backend or dashboard stack just to get desktop distribution. The better structure is: 1. switchboard-core: protocol, orchestration, adapters, persistence, retention, export, decision memory, and sandbox abstractions; 2. local-server-host: config discovery, data-directory setup, migrations, PID/single-instance behavior, dynamic port binding, startup phase reporting, health endpoints, log setup, graceful shutdown; 3. desktop-launcher: OS-native executable/app bundle that starts or attaches to the host, shows immediate startup state, opens the UI in the user's browser for MVP, exposes tray/menu actions for open app, restart backend, open logs, open data folder, export, and quit.

I accept Gemini's point on port collisions. A packaged app should not assume a fixed port. The host should bind to an available localhost port, write the active port to a runtime file or hand it directly to the launcher, and ensure browser/dashboard URLs use that actual port. Single-instance behavior should route a second launch to the already-running instance rather than starting another backend.

I partially accept Gemini's packaging critique. PyInstaller one-file can have a cold-start extraction penalty and should not be the default if speed is a priority. But saying PyInstaller is categorically wrong is too broad because one-dir packaging behaves differently and may still be useful for internal builds. The packaging comparison should explicitly benchmark PyInstaller one-dir, Nuitka, Python embeddable distribution, PyOxidizer, and Briefcase against cold start, signing/notarization friction, bundle size, update behavior, and supportability. For the shell, I accept the argument that a browser/tray launcher is the better MVP than pywebview. A webview can be revisited later if the default-browser experience is unacceptable.

I accept Claude's point that external CLI adapters are the load-bearing wall. The packaged app should start even when Codex, Claude Code, or Gemini CLIs are missing or unauthenticated. Missing adapters should be reported in an Agents/Readiness panel with clear install/auth guidance and should disable only those seats. OpenRouter/API-backed seats and fake/test seats can remain available if configured. Startup should not block on CLI checks; readiness checks should run asynchronously after the UI is usable. This preserves operability and avoids turning distribution into a brittle all-or-nothing installer.

I accept the startup-measurement critique. The architecture should not assume lazy adapter construction is the main bottleneck. The first permissioned implementation pass should baseline cold and warm launch: launcher visible time, Python process start, imports, config load, DB/WAL setup, orphan reaper, sandbox cleanup, server listening, /health OK, first dashboard render, and async adapter readiness. Until measured, practical target budgets should be treated as provisional: launcher visible under 500 ms, /health ready under 2 s warm and under 5 s cold on a normal developer laptop, dashboard interactive under 3 s warm and under 7 s cold, and adapter readiness checks completed asynchronously within roughly 5-10 s without blocking core UI.

I accept Claude's config-path critique. Packaging is blocked until mutable state is moved out of the install/app bundle. The host should resolve OS-specific directories: on Windows, config/data/logs under a user profile location such as %APPDATA% or %LOCALAPPDATA% depending on roaming needs; on macOS, under ~/Library/Application Support/<app name> with logs also available through a user-accessible logs path. The existing cwd/config.yaml behavior should become a development fallback or explicit override, not the packaged default. Migration should copy or import existing config, task DB, exports, and decision records once, leave the original intact, and record the migration in logs.

Operability impact: this structure strengthens observability through startup phases, health/readiness endpoints, runtime-port reporting, and user-accessible logs. It strengthens durability and recoverability by separating immutable bundled app files from mutable DB/config/log/export state. It preserves audit trail, retention, and export if task history and decision records remain in stable user-data paths and export remains accessible from the launcher/UI. It does increase build and release complexity, especially for Windows/macOS signing and dependency readiness UX, but that complexity is directly tied to distribution and is bounded by keeping the backend and dashboard architecture largely intact.

Final structure recommendation: do not pursue a self-contained zero-dependency exe/dmg as the immediate promise. Ship a one-click desktop distribution that bundles the Switchboard backend and dashboard, starts fast enough, degrades gracefully when external agent CLIs are missing, and clearly reports readiness. Treat fully bundled third-party CLIs, embedded webview, and auto-update as later decisions after the host layer, path model, and startup measurements are solid.

A much more robust 'thin shell' for an MVP is a lightweight system tray executable (e.g., using Go, Rust/Tauri, or PyStray) that orchestrates the backend and opens the user's default OS browser to the local port. Finally, running a local web server introduces port collision risks; the host layer must dynamically bind to an available port and pass it to the UI shell.
disagreements.primary_position: The best target architecture is a local-service desktop app: keep the existing FastAPI/orchestration core, add a small host layer for startup, paths, health, logging, and supervision, and ship a thin desktop launcher/tray app that opens the local UI. The critical blockers are not the UI wrapper itself but external CLI adapter dependencies, config/data path migration, dynamic port handling, and measured cold-start behavior.

disagreements.consultant_position:
Strong direction on the three-layer architecture and operability-first thinking. The principle of separating app bundle from mutable user data, explicit startup phases, and health checks before UI load is sound and aligns with the Charter's operability principle. However, the proposal underestimates three critical blockers that will dominate the actual implementation:

**1. External CLI dependency is the real blocker, not packaging.** The entire adapter layer (codex_adapter, gemini_adapter, claude_adapter) wraps subprocess calls to external CLIs. These are not Python dependencies — you cannot bundle them into an exe/dmg. The proposal mentions this as "a high-severity risk" but then moves forward without a concrete solution. For the distribution goal to work, you need: (a) a strategy for detecting/checking CLI availability at runtime, (b) explicit degradation modes when a CLI is missing, (c) either bundled CLI shims or a clear user-facing guide that says "install Codex separately first." None of these are addressed. PyInstaller won't solve this — it only bundles Python. The adapter layer is the load-bearing wall for the packaging path, and it needs its own design pass.

**2. Startup bottleneck is unidentified.** The proposal calls for lazy loading adapters and deferred initialization, but adapter instantiation is probably not the critical path. Reading the source: `agent_registry.init_registry()` and `register_openrouter_models()` just instantiate adapter objects; they don't I/O. The real startup cost is likely (a) Python import time (FastAPI, Pydantic, etc.), (b) SQLite open + WAL setup + busy_timeout negotiation, (c) orphan reaper scan (DB query), (d) sandbox cleanup (filesystem walk). Defer adapter init, sure, but that's maybe 5–15% of startup time. Without a measurement pass (which the proposal acknowledges is not possible in read-only mode), you risk doing a three-layer refactor for gains that aren't there. Suggest: measure first. Cold start a current uvicorn instance and profile with `py-spy` or `cProfile` to identify the actual bottleneck. That data should drive the architecture, not the other way around.

**3. Config path refactoring is deferred but blocks packaging.** The app currently reads `config.yaml` from cwd or `SWITCHBOARD_CONFIG` env var. For a packaged app to work, config must move to OS-appropriate user-data directories: `~/Library/Application Support/AI Switchboard/` on macOS, `%APPDATA%/AI Switchboard/` on Windows. The proposal says "config and secrets should live in OS-appropriate user data locations" but doesn't specify the implementation. `app/config.py:load_config()` has to change, startup has to handle config discovery and migration, and the installer has to seed initial config. This is a real refactor that the proposal does not scope.

**Strong agreement:**
- Three-layer split (core/host/shell) reduces coupling and is correct long-term.
- Explicit startup phases with `phase_status` is excellent for observability and user feedback.
- Health endpoint before dashboard load is the right optimization priority.
- Preserving exports and decision memory as externally-auditable artifacts is essential — packaging must not hide those.
- Conservative path (PyInstaller or pywebview, not Electron) is pragmatic.

**Risks the proposal identifies but doesn't resolve:**
- "External agent adapters may depend on installed CLIs" — yes, and this is the blocker for self-contained exe/dmg, not a secondary concern.
- "Packaging Python apps can accidentally place mutable state inside install directories" — true, and this is more likely without explicit path design upfront.

**Questions for the primary:**
- Before the three-layer refactor, can you baseline actual cold-start time? Is it actually slow, or is the goal "more user-friendly than running a command"?
- If external CLIs are unavailable, should Switchboard degrade gracefully (OpenRouter-only mode, fake adapter, or hard fail)? This shapes the adapter architecture.
- Is the MVP distribution really "exe/dmg that ship with zero setup," or is "Python backend + one-click launcher that assumes CLIs are on PATH" acceptable as a first step?
- Where should config live after packaging? (This determines app/config.py and installer design.)
disagreements.primary_position: The best target architecture is a local-service desktop app: keep the existing FastAPI/orchestration core, add a small host layer for startup, paths, health, logging, and supervision, and ship a thin desktop launcher/tray app that opens the local UI. The critical blockers are not the UI wrapper itself but external CLI adapter dependencies, config/data path migration, dynamic port handling, and measured cold-start behavior.

recommended_actions.description: Adopt the three-layer desktop distribution architecture: switchboard-core, local-server-host, and desktop-launcher/browser shell, while preserving the existing FastAPI backend and static dashboard for the first packaged release.

payload.mvp_shell: native launcher or tray app that opens the default browser

recommended_actions.description: Define the local-server-host contract: OS-specific config/data/log paths, dynamic localhost port binding, single-instance behavior, startup phase reporting, fast health endpoint, readiness endpoint, graceful shutdown, and user-visible failure states.

Create an adapter dependency/readiness model so missing Codex, Claude Code, or Gemini CLIs do not prevent the app from starting; unavailable seats should be disabled with clear install/auth guidance while available API-backed seats continue to work.

payload.startup_behavior: asynchronous readiness checks after core UI loads

recommended_actions.description: In a later permissioned pass, measure current cold and warm startup before refactoring so startup work targets the real bottlenecks.

Benchmark packaging options against startup speed and operability: Python embeddable distribution, Nuitka, PyOxidizer, Briefcase, and PyInstaller one-dir; avoid PyInstaller one-file as the default until its cold-start cost is proven acceptable.
Design config/data migration from cwd/config.yaml and existing local project paths into OS user-data locations, preserving original files and recording migration evidence in logs.

migration_requirements: support explicit config override for development

recommended_actions.description: If Glen accepts this direction, create a decision record for desktop distribution architecture including the Operability Impact field required by the charter.

risks.description: External CLI-backed adapters cannot be assumed to work inside a packaged exe/dmg. Without a readiness/degradation model, the desktop app may fail for users who have not separately installed or authenticated Codex, Claude Code, or Gemini tooling.

No command execution is granted in this task, so all startup bottleneck claims remain static hypotheses. Actual cold-start profiling requires can_run_commands permission before implementation priorities are final.
Moving config, logs, databases, exports, and decision records to OS user-data paths can break continuity if migration is not explicit, non-destructive, and logged.
A fixed localhost port can collide with another process and make the packaged app appear broken. Dynamic port binding and single-instance handoff are required.
Packaging choices affect operability. One-file bundle extraction, signing/notarization failures, opaque crash logs, or mutable state inside app bundles can undermine startup speed and recoverability.
A browser-based MVP is more robust than an embedded webview but may feel less like a native app. That is an acceptable first tradeoff if startup, logging, export, and adapter readiness are stronger.
Large dashboard JS/CSS files may affect first render and bundle size, but they should be measured before becoming a refactor target.
